Task Management and Cleanup (tmc/task.h)
[Tilera Multicore Components (TMC&tm;) Library]

Detailed Description

Task management and cleanup operations.

Starting the Shepherd

A "shepherd" is a special process that can watch other processes, tracking both the forking of child processes and the creation of threads, and treating the resulting set of processes as a single "application". If any process crashes (or exits with an exit code between 128 and 255), the shepherd kills all of the other processes in the same application.

A shepherd can be started by running /bin/shepherd, with optional --tile arguments, followed by an initial executable, and its arguments. This shepherd exits once the application has exited, with an exit status based on how the processes in the application exited.

A monitored shepherd can be started by running /bin/shepherd with arguments telling it how to communicate with a tile-monitor process on the host. A monitored shepherd helps the monitor manage (and debug) processes, and their consoles.

Each process launched by the monitor becomes the first process in a new application.

A monitored shepherd can support the use of the "FUSE" filesystem, which exports the host filesystem to Tilera Linux, with the cooperation of the monitor.

For more information on tile-monitor, see the shipped tile-monitor man page, also available as the "Tile Processor Monitor" reference in the "Target Platform Tools" section.

Interacting with the Shepherd

The tmc_task_has_monitor() function can be used to determine if the current process has a monitored shepherd as an ancestor, and the tmc_task_has_shepherd() function can be used to determine if the current process has any shepherd as an ancestor. In the current implementation, when these functions return true, the shepherd starts watching the process (if it is not already doing so).

The tmc_task_watch_forked_children() function tells the shepherd whether forked children should be watched and considered to be part of an application. By default, this is false, and should remain false, except when actually forking interesting children, to avoid confusing system() (and such). For example:

 int watch_forked_children = tmc_task_watch_forked_children(1);
 pid_t pid = fork();
 if (pid == 0)
   run_child();
 tmc_task_watch_forked_children(watch_forked_children);

The tmc_task_assume_impending_exec() function can be used in much the same way as tmc_task_watch_forked_children(), to let the shepherd know that forked children will be calling execv() (or similar) sufficiently quickly that attempting to debug them before they do so would only annoy the user. Note that this is only necessary if the shepherd will be watching the forked child. For example:

 int watch_forked_children = tmc_task_watch_forked_children(1);
 int assume_impending_exec = tmc_task_assume_impending_exec(1);
 pid_t pid = fork();
 if (pid == 0)
   exec_child();
 tmc_task_watch_forked_children(watch_forked_children);
 tmc_task_assume_impending_exec(assume_impending_exec);

The tmc_task_monitor_console() function creates pipes to be used as the stdin, stdout, and stderr streams for the current process, and passes the other ends of the pipes to the (monitored) shepherd. The monitor displays any data read from the stdout and stderr pipes, and supplies data to be written to the stdin pipe.

Functions

void tmc_task_die (const char *format,...)
 Write an error message to stderr, and then call exit(128).
int tmc_task_has_monitor (void)
 Determine if the current process has a monitored shepherd.
int tmc_task_has_shepherd (void)
 Determine if the current process has a shepherd.
int tmc_task_watch_forked_children (int flag)
 Set the "watch forked children" flag.
int tmc_task_assume_impending_exec (int flag)
 Set whether or not the calling process will quickly follow a call to fork() with a call to exec().
int tmc_task_terminate_app (void)
 Terminate the "application" to which the current process belongs.
int tmc_task_monitor_console (void)
 Create stdin, stdout, and stderr pipes for the current process and ask the shepherd to monitor them.
pid_t tmc_task_gettid (void)
 Get the "task id" for the current thread (aka "gettid()").

Function Documentation

void tmc_task_die ( const char *  format,
  ... 
)

Write an error message to stderr, and then call exit(128).

The message is written to stderr, preceded by "ERROR: ", and followed by a newline.

If the current process is being watched by a shepherd, the shepherd will kill any other processes in the same application.

Parameters:
format The format string.
... The arguments for the format string.
Examples:
mica/queue/app.c, mpipe/chaining/app.c, mpipe/forward/app.c, mpipe/forward/app_gx8072.c, mpipe/ingress/app.c, mpipe/loopback/app.c, mpipe/multi_rules/app.c, tmc/memory_perf/memory.c, tmc/multi_app/app.c, tmc/multi_tile/multi_tile.c, tmc/queue/bench_one_to_one.c, trio/gxpci_c2c/c2c_send.c, trio/gxpci_host_nic/gxpci_echo_drv_module.c, trio/gxpci_host_pq/host.c, trio/gxpci_mmi/mmi.c, and trio/push_dma/app.c.

int tmc_task_has_monitor ( void   ) 

Determine if the current process has a monitored shepherd.

Currently, if this function returns true, the shepherd starts watching the current process, if it is not already doing so.

If this function returns false, then various other functions have no useful effect.

Returns:
1 for true, 0 for false.

int tmc_task_has_shepherd ( void   ) 

Determine if the current process has a shepherd.

Currently, if this function returns true, the shepherd starts watching the current process, if it is not already doing so.

If this function returns false, then various other functions will not have any useful effect.

Returns:
1 for true, 0 for false.

int tmc_task_watch_forked_children ( int  flag  ) 

Set the "watch forked children" flag.

This flag defaults to false, and, if true, the shepherd, if any, watches any forked processes.

Parameters:
flag The desired flag value.
Returns:
-1 (setting errno) on error, or the previous value (0 or 1).
Examples:
tmc/multi_app/app.c, and tmc/multi_tile/multi_tile.c.

int tmc_task_assume_impending_exec ( int  flag  ) 

Set whether or not the calling process will quickly follow a call to fork() with a call to exec().

This flag defaults to false, and, if true, the shepherd, if any, avoids creating a debugger until execv() (or similar) has been called.

Parameters:
flag The desired flag value.
Returns:
-1 (setting errno) on error, or the previous value (0 or 1).

int tmc_task_terminate_app ( void   ) 

Terminate the "application" to which the current process belongs.

This causes all processes in the same application to be killed with SIGKILL.

Returns:
-1 if tmc_task_has_shepherd() is false, else never returns.

int tmc_task_monitor_console ( void   ) 

Create stdin, stdout, and stderr pipes for the current process and ask the shepherd to monitor them.

This function does nothing unless tmc_task_has_monitor() is true.

Note that once this function has been called successfully, stdout and stderr from the process are no longer deterministically ordered relative to the stdout and stderr of other processes, due to the actual mechanism by which the pipes are monitored.

Returns:
-1 (setting error) on error, 1 (setting errno to EINVAL) if already called successfully, or zero.

pid_t tmc_task_gettid ( void   ) 

Get the "task id" for the current thread (aka "gettid()").

For a single threaded process, or the initial process of a multi-threaded process, this is the same as the process id.

Although technically glibc does not guarantee that a given pthread will stay bound to a single Linux task, in the current implementation that is true, and there are no plans to change it.

The task ID can be helpful for various things, including SIGEV_THREAD_ID with timer_create(), sched_setaffinity() or sched_getaffinity() calls, and fcntl() F_SETOWN/F_GETOWN commands. In addition, it can be used to identify threads in "ps".

Returns:
The task id.
